planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rose acacia spreads by suckers and can form colonies — manage suckering by root-pruning with a spade around the base annually if a formal clump is desired. The brittle, hairy branches are easily damaged in wind and ice storms. In poor, dry soils it grows more slowly and spreads less aggressively.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rose acacia is a native Appalachian shrub producing some of the most spectacular rose-pink to deep-rose, large (1-inch) pea flowers of any native leguminous plant — carried in pendant clusters in May and June that are ornamentally stunning and among the richest pink flowers of any hardy shrub. The entire plant is covered with stiff, bristly, reddish hairs (hence 'bristly locust') that give it an interesting texture. As a suckering, colony-forming shrub, it is excellent for covering dry, eroded banks and slopes where it spreads to form an effective, beautiful, ground-stabilizing mass. The brittle, spreading stems are easily damaged by wind and winter ice, and it can look disheveled in formal plantings, but in naturalistic and restoration settings it is outstanding. A nitrogen-fixing legume, it improves lean, poor soils. Hummingbirds are strongly attracted to the large, pendant rose flowers. Sometimes grafted onto black locust rootstock to create a small standard tree form that eliminates the suckering habit.

planting.note: Direct-sow after last frost in moist to wet, sandy or loamy soil in full sun to part shade; yellow sensitive plant grows naturally in moist low areas, roadsides, and disturbed ground along the Gulf Coast and tolerates seasonally wet soils. It self-sows readily and will naturalize in appropriate conditions in zones 9–11.

directions: Direct-sow or allow to naturalize in moist to seasonally wet soil in full sun to part shade; yellow sensitive plant is a native Gulf Coast legume found naturally in moist, disturbed areas, roadsides, and low-lying fields from Florida to Texas, producing cheerful bright-yellow, ball-shaped puffball flowers (mimosa-like heads) in summer and fall and notable for its leaves that fold inward instantly when touched, shaken, or disturbed — exactly like the tropical sensitive plant (Mimosa pudica). The folding leaf response — a phenomenon called thigmonasty — is a source of endless fascination to children and adults alike. As a nitrogen-fixing native legume, it improves poor, moist soils. It is an annual in the Gulf Coast area but self-sows reliably in conditions it likes. The small, puffy, yellow flower heads are attractive to bees and other pollinators. An excellent plant for naturalistic, low-maintenance areas of the Gulf Coast garden where its seasonal moist soil requirements and self-seeding habit can be accommodated.

planting.note: Scarify seeds before sowing in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; cream wild indigo is exceptionally drought-tolerant and thrives in lean, sandy, dry prairie soils. It develops a deep taproot and cannot be transplanted once established — site carefully from the start. It is one of the earliest-blooming Baptisia species, often blooming before B. australis in the spring prairie sequence.

directions: Sow scarified seeds in lean, well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; cream wild indigo is the earliest-blooming species of Baptisia, native to the sandy prairies and oak savannas of the central United States and producing large clusters of creamy-white to pale-ivory pea flowers in April and early May — before most other wild indigos bloom — along horizontally spreading, low, sprawling stems. The low, wide-spreading habit (wider than tall) is characteristic and distinctive — unlike the upright blue wild indigo, cream wild indigo lies low and spreads outward, forming a wide clump that can reach 3 feet across at maturity. The large, showy bracts beneath the flower clusters are persistent and ornamental. The large, inflated seed pods follow the flowers and are ornamental through fall and winter. A specialist plant for the hottest, driest, most challenging full-sun positions where its prairie-adapted drought tolerance is an asset, and its early bloom is welcome in the spring garden before most prairie perennials have begun.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or transplant small seedlings in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; dwarf false indigo is shorter and more compact than the common leadplant (A. canescens) and better suited to smaller gardens and rock garden settings. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it improves poor, lean soils.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry soil in full sun; dwarf false indigo is a compact, low-growing native shrub of the central Great Plains and northern prairie, producing arching, slender spikes of small, deep violet-purple flowers with prominent golden stamens in June and July — a striking color combination that makes this modest, low plant surprisingly showy in bloom. The finely divided, gray-green, pinnate foliage has a somewhat silvery appearance similar to its taller relative leadplant (A. canescens) and is attractive throughout the growing season. At only 1–2 feet tall, it is more usable in smaller gardens and rock gardens than its taller relatives. As a nitrogen-fixing prairie legume, it is extremely drought-tolerant and adapted to the lean, dry, sandy-loam soils of the northern Great Plains. The flowers are excellent for native bees, particularly specialist bees that depend on Amorpha for pollen. A useful low, spreading native for dry, lean garden positions where more demanding plants struggle.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; bladder senna is native to dry, rocky, limestone hillsides of southern Europe and the Mediterranean and requires excellent drainage. It tolerates drought, poor soil, alkaline conditions, and air pollution that defeat most ornamental shrubs. Prune hard in late winter to keep compact and prevent legginess.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; bladder senna is a large, fast-growing Mediterranean shrub notable for its unusual seed pods — large, inflated, papery bladder-like structures that balloon out to 1–2 inches, becoming translucent when fully ripe and resembling little lanterns or balloons hanging from the branches. Children delight in popping these pods with a 'pop.' The pods develop from yellow to orange-red pea flowers that bloom in racemes from May through September, giving an unusually long bloom season for a legume shrub. The attractive, blue-gray pinnate foliage is pleasant throughout the season. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it thrives in the poorest, most alkaline soils and is one of the most tolerant ornamental shrubs for urban conditions including compacted alkaline soil, air pollution, and drought. It grows rapidly and can become leggy — hard annual pruning maintains a compact, bushy form. Noted in historical gardens for the curiosity of its inflatable pods.
